before: Henry VIII's second wife, she was executed for treason in 1536, with the circumstances of her marriage regarded as a key catalyst for the English Reformation.
proposed: This second wife of Henry VIII was executed for treason in 1536; Henry's pursuit of their marriage, which required a break from Rome, helped spark the English Reformation.
comment: Rewrite is a big improvement. Once piece of feedback: the second part of the question is too indirect and choppy. Suggestion: "Henry's pursuit of their marriage required him to defy the Pope, which helped spark the English Reformation.

before: The final emperor of the Julio-Claudian dynasty, this ruler reigned from 54 to 68 AD before dying by suicide; his mother, the influential Agrippina the Younger, was murdered on his orders.
proposed: This ruler reigned from 54 to 68 AD as the last Julio-Claudian emperor, dying by suicide — and had his own mother, Agrippina the Younger, murdered on his orders.
comment: Better but not quite: This ruler reigned from 54 to 68 AD as the last Julio-Claudian emperor before dying by suicide; his notorious acts included ordering the murder of his mother, Agrippina the Younger

before: This author devised the Three Laws of Robotics across his science-fiction stories.
proposed: This author devised the Three Laws of Robotics.
comment: I don't agree with this one. 'science fiction stories' adds some color, especially to such a short question

before: Count Vronsky's affair with this Tolstoy title heroine begins and ends with a death at a train station.
proposed: This Tolstoy title heroine's affair with Count Vronsky begins and ends with a death at a train station.

before: In this author's novels, 42 is the Answer to the Ultimate Question of Life, the Universe, and Everything.
proposed: This author's novels declare 42 to be the Answer to the Ultimate Question of Life, the Universe, and Everything.

before: The greatest novelist of the Victorian era by many accounts, this English author left school at 12 when his father was imprisoned for debt, forcing him to work in a boot-blacking factory.
proposed: This Victorian author of 'Oliver Twist' and 'Great Expectations' left school at 12 when his father was imprisoned for debt, forcing him to work in a boot-blacking factory.
comment: This one makes the question significantly easier. I agree with the comment that 'Opening superlative 'The greatest novelist … by many accounts' is abstract and buries the subject' but the solution changes the question's difficulty substantially. Could either: 1. Just say 'Before becoming one of the greatest authors of his era, this English writer left school at 12 after his father was imprisoned for debt and was forced to work in a boot-blacking factory. 2. Replace with a difficult more obscure fact

before: Agatha Christie's most famous and longest-running fictional creation, this fastidious Belgian detective relies on his 'little grey cells' and appears in 33 novels and 51 short stories.
proposed: This fastidious Belgian detective relies on his 'little grey cells' to solve cases across 33 Agatha Christie novels and 51 short stories.
comment: Good, but should be past tense (relied) which was also a problem in the original clue

before: An example of the literary nonsense genre, this 1865 Lewis Carroll children's novel follows a young girl who tumbles down a rabbit hole into a fantasy world of anthropomorphic creatures.
proposed: This 1865 Lewis Carroll children's novel follows a young girl who tumbles down a rabbit hole into a fantasy world of anthropomorphic creatures.

before: Though he saw himself primarily as a poet, this English Victorian author achieved fame through his novels set in the fictional county of Wessex, including 'Tess of the d'Urbervilles.'
proposed: This English Victorian author set his novels in the fictional county of Wessex — including 'Tess of the d'Urbervilles' — though he considered himself primarily a poet.
comment: I actually like the before here more. It's okay to bury the subject at times when the lede is very clear like it is here. The problem with the original clue is that 'including 'Tess of the d'Urbervilles.' is comma spliced in which isn't really fixed here either. Proposal: Though he saw himself primarily as a poet, this English Victorian author achieved fame through novels such as 'Tess of the d'Urbervilles' set in the fictional county of Wessex

before: The American novelist who coined the term 'Jazz Age' in a 1922 short story collection achieved transient success in the 1920s but received critical acclaim only after his death.
proposed: This American novelist coined the term 'Jazz Age' and wrote 'The Great Gatsby,' though critical acclaim came only after his death.
comment: Fixes one problem and creates others by making it a lot easier by adding the great gatsby and comma splicing onto the end (also the comma is not part of the title's name and should fall outside the quotes(. Combining them: This American novelist coined the term 'Jazz Age' in a 1922 short story collection and achieved transient success during his lifetime, but only received critical acclaim after his death.

before: Concern over this company's bankruptcy centered on the genetic data stored in its databases.
proposed: Concern over this company's bankruptcy centered on its customers' genetic data.

before: In 'Top Gun,' Maverick starts a rivalry with this rival pilot played by Val Kilmer.
proposed: In 'Top Gun,' Maverick clashes with this pilot played by Val Kilmer.
comment: Right to suggest a fix, but the better fix is just to remove 'rival' since 'starts a rivalry' is better than clashes.

before: This Greek philosopher held that 'eudaimonia,' or flourishing, is the highest human good.
proposed: Author of the 'Nicomachean Ethics,' this ancient thinker held that 'eudaimonia' — human flourishing — is the highest good.
comment: This isn't an improvement and makes the question easier

before: This ethical stance prioritizes actions that benefit the long-run future and far-future generations.
proposed: This ethical stance holds that improving the lives of people in the far future should be humanity's top priority.

before: Romanized from the Greek Nike during the First Punic War, she came to symbolize Rome's hegemony and right to rule; a deified abstraction with virtually no mythology of her own, she left behind a name now borne by far more women than worshippers.
proposed: Romanized from the Greek Nike during the First Punic War, she symbolized Rome's imperial hegemony; a deified abstraction with virtually no mythology, she left behind a name borne by far more women than worshippers.
comment: I prefer the original -- the changes break up the flow/elegance of the question

before: This constant, approximately 6.022 x 10^23, is how many particles are contained in one mole of a substance.
proposed: This constant, approximately 6.022 × 10^23, counts the particles in one mole of a substance.

before: The wings of these tiny birds can beat up to about 80 times per second.
proposed: The wings of these tiny birds beat up to 80 times per second.

before: Paul, a member of this eight-armed species, correctly called all eight matches he was given at the 2010 World Cup.
proposed: Paul, a member of this eight-armed species, correctly predicted all eight of his 2010 World Cup picks.

before: Native to much of South America, it is the largest living rodent.
proposed: Native to South America, this animal is the largest living rodent.

before: Produced by certain molluscs to line the inside of their shells, this iridescent material is the same substance that forms a pearl.
proposed: Produced by certain molluscs to line their shells, this iridescent material also forms pearls.

before: This biome of conifers — pines, spruces, and larches — is the world’s largest land biome, ringing the subarctic across Canada, Scandinavia, and Russia.
proposed: The world's largest land biome, this belt of conifers — pines, spruces, and larches — rings the subarctic across Canada, Scandinavia, and Russia.

before: This Hall of Famer stole a record 1,406 bases, far more than any other player in MLB history.
proposed: This Hall of Famer stole 1,406 career bases, the MLB record.
comment: Disagree, it's important that this is a runaway record and for a short question, the extra words improve flow

before: This Kenner toy introduced in 1963 originally used a pair of ordinary incandescent light bulbs as its heat source; Kenner sold 500,000 units in the first year of production.
proposed: Introduced by Kenner in 1963, this toy used a pair of incandescent light bulbs as its heat source and sold 500,000 units in the first year.

before: This line of collectible plush toys, created by Hong Kong illustrator Kasing Lung and sold exclusively by Pop Mart in sealed 'blind box' packaging that hides which figure is inside, became a global phenomenon in 2024.
proposed: This line of collectible plush toys, created by Hong Kong illustrator Kasing Lung and sold by Pop Mart in sealed 'blind box' packaging, became a global phenomenon in 2024.

before: In this winter Olympic sport, teams of four players take turns sliding polished granite stones toward a circular target — called 'the house' — on a sheet of ice, while two teammates sweep with brooms to guide the stone's path.
proposed: In this winter Olympic sport, four-player teams take turns sliding polished granite stones toward a circular target — the house — on a sheet of ice, while two teammates sweep with brooms to guide the stone's path.

before: Borrowed directly from French, this word names a listless weariness and dissatisfaction that arises from boredom or a lack of excitement.
proposed: Borrowed directly from French, this word names a listless weariness and dissatisfaction arising from boredom.
comment: Not redundant, but agree that arising from is better than 'that arises from'

before: The surname of an 18th-century German physician who claimed to heal patients through 'animal magnetism' is the origin of this word, meaning 'to hold someone so spellbound that they seem entranced.'
proposed: This verb, meaning to hold someone so spellbound they seem entranced, comes from an 18th-century German physician who claimed to heal patients through 'animal magnetism.'

before: Coined by the writer Sylvia Wright in 1954, this term for a misheard word or phrase — most often a misinterpreted song lyric — arose when she reinterpreted a ballad's line 'laid him on the green' as the name of a slain noblewoman.
proposed: This term for a misheard word or phrase — most often a song lyric — was coined by writer Sylvia Wright in 1954, who named it after mishearing a line of a Scottish ballad as the name of a slain noblewoman.
